walkthrough.title: Remove Exploding Kittens and Defuses
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Exploding Kittens set up. First, go through the entire deck and pull out all the Exploding Kitten cards and all the Defuse cards. Set them aside in two separate piles. The Exploding Kittens have a picture of a kitten about to explode, and the Defuse cards show things like laser pointers and belly rubs. Everything else stays in the main deck.

walkthrough.title: Deal Defuse Cards
walkthrough.text: Now take the Defuse pile. Deal exactly one Defuse card face down to each player. This is your lifeline. It is the only thing that saves you if you draw an Exploding Kitten, so keep it safe. Put any leftover Defuse cards back into the main deck.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the main deck, which still has the leftover Defuse cards mixed in but no Exploding Kittens yet. Deal seven cards face down to each player. Combined with the Defuse card you already received, everyone should now have a hand of eight cards. Go ahead and look at your hand, but keep it hidden from other players.

walkthrough.title: Insert Exploding Kittens
walkthrough.text: Now count the number of players and grab one fewer Exploding Kitten card. So if you have four players, insert three Exploding Kittens into the remaining deck. If you have two players, insert just one. Put any extra Exploding Kittens back in the box. Shuffle the deck thoroughly and place it face down in the center of the table. You are ready to play!

summary.title: Remove Exploding Kittens and Defuses
bullets: Remove all 4 Exploding Kitten cards from the deck
Remove all 6 Defuse cards from the deck
Set both piles aside separately

summary.title: Deal Defuse Cards
bullets: Deal 1 Defuse card to each player
Shuffle remaining Defuse cards back into the main deck

summary.title: Deal Starting Hands
bullets: Shuffle the deck (includes leftover Defuses, no Exploding Kittens)
Deal 7 cards to each player → 8-card starting hand (7 + 1 Defuse)

summary.title: Insert Exploding Kittens
bullets: Insert (players - 1) Exploding Kitten cards into the deck
Return unused Exploding Kittens to the box
Shuffle deck, place face-down in center → ready to play

walkthrough.title: How a Turn Works
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you may play as many action cards as you want from your hand, or none at all. When you are done playing cards, you must draw one card from the top of the draw pile. Drawing a card always ends your turn. You cannot skip the draw unless you play a Skip card or an Attack card.

walkthrough.title: Drawing an Exploding Kitten
walkthrough.text: If the card you draw is an Exploding Kitten, you are in trouble. Show it to everyone immediately. If you have a Defuse card in your hand, play it to survive. Discard the Defuse card and then secretly put the Exploding Kitten back anywhere in the draw pile. You can put it on top, on the bottom, or anywhere in the middle. Do not let anyone see where you place it.

walkthrough.title: Elimination
walkthrough.text: If you draw an Exploding Kitten and you do not have a Defuse card, you are eliminated from the game. Discard all your cards face down and sit out for the rest of the round. The last player still alive wins the game. There is no scoring, just pure survival.

walkthrough.title: Skip and Attack Cards
walkthrough.text: A Skip card ends your turn immediately without drawing a card. If you are under an Attack and owe two turns, a Skip only cancels one of them, so you still take the second turn. An Attack card also ends your turn without drawing, but the next player in order must take two turns in a row. Attacks stack, so if the next player is already under an Attack, they now owe four turns total.

walkthrough.title: See the Future and Shuffle
walkthrough.text: See the Future lets you privately peek at the top three cards of the draw pile without changing their order. This is incredibly useful for knowing whether it is safe to draw. Shuffle lets you randomize the entire draw pile. Use Shuffle when you know something dangerous is on top, or after someone places an Exploding Kitten back into the deck.

walkthrough.title: Favor Card
walkthrough.text: When you play a Favor card, point at any other player. That player must give you one card from their hand. Here is the catch: they get to choose which card to give you. They will almost certainly give you the least useful card they have, but any information or card you pull from an opponent is still valuable.

walkthrough.title: Cat Card Combos
walkthrough.text: Cat cards have no special power on their own. Instead, you use them in matching sets. Play two matching cat cards as a pair, and you get to steal one random card from any opponent. They fan their hand face down and you pick blindly. Play three matching cat cards as a triple, and you get to name a specific card and demand it from any opponent. If they have it, they must hand it over. If they do not have it, you get nothing.

walkthrough.title: The Nope Card
walkthrough.text: A Nope card can be played at any time, even on someone else's turn. It cancels the action of any card just played. You can Nope a Skip, an Attack, a Favor, a See the Future, a Shuffle, or a cat card combo. You cannot Nope an Exploding Kitten or a Defuse card. Nope cards can also chain. If someone Nopes your card, another player can Nope the Nope, which makes the original card resolve. An odd number of Nopes cancels the action and an even number lets it happen.

summary.title: How a Turn Works
bullets: Play any number of action cards (optional)
Draw 1 card from draw pile (mandatory, ends turn)
Cannot skip the draw without Skip or Attack

summary.title: Drawing an Exploding Kitten
bullets: Show it to all players immediately
Play Defuse → discard Defuse, secretly reinsert kitten anywhere in deck
Placement is secret — top, bottom, or anywhere in the middle

summary.title: Elimination
bullets: No Defuse → eliminated, discard all cards
Last player alive wins — no scoring
Eliminated players sit out the rest of the game

summary.title: Skip and Attack Cards
bullets: Skip → end turn without drawing (cancels only 1 turn if under Attack)
Attack → end turn without drawing, next player takes 2 turns
Attacks stack: 2 → 4 turns if chained

summary.title: See the Future and Shuffle
bullets: See the Future → peek at top 3 cards privately, don't reorder
Shuffle → randomize the entire draw pile
Combo: See the Future to spot danger → Shuffle to remove it

summary.title: Favor Card
bullets: Target any player → they must give you 1 card
They choose which card to give (not you)
Still valuable for information and depleting opponents' hands

summary.title: Cat Card Combos
bullets: Pair (2 matching) → steal 1 random card from any opponent
Triple (3 matching) → name a specific card, demand it from opponent
Cat cards have no individual power — only work in sets

summary.title: The Nope Card
bullets: Play anytime, even on others' turns — cancels any action card
Cannot Nope Exploding Kittens or Defuse cards
Nope chains: odd # = canceled, even # = resolves

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Table
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice game with three example players. Everyone has been dealt their eight cards, including one Defuse, and two Exploding Kittens are lurking somewhere in the draw pile. Let's see how things unfold.

walkthrough.title: Player One's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player One looks at their hand and sees a See the Future, a Skip, two Taco Cat cards, a Shuffle, a Nope, a Defuse, and a Favor. The deck is still thick and safe, so Player One decides not to play anything and simply draws a card. They pull a Beard Cat. Nice and safe. Turn over.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Gets Greedy
walkthrough.text: Player Two has a Defuse card and a Favor. They really want more cards, so they play the Favor card targeting Player Three. Player Three grudgingly hands over a Hairy Potato Cat. Player Two then draws a card from the deck. No explosion. But here is the thing: Player Two just burned an action card to gain one mediocre cat card. Not a great trade early in the game when the deck is thick and the real danger is far away.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Save Favors for Later
walkthrough.text: Using Favor cards early is wasteful because you get whatever the opponent wants to dump on you. Save your Favor for later in the game when hands are smaller and opponents might be forced to give up a Defuse or a critical Nope. Timing is everything in this game.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Draws Safely
walkthrough.text: Player Three plays it cool and just draws a card. Nothing special. When the deck is fat and the odds of hitting an Exploding Kitten are low, there is no reason to waste your action cards. The early game is all about conserving resources for when things get intense.

walkthrough.title: The Deck Gets Thin
walkthrough.text: Several rounds pass. The deck is getting noticeably smaller now. One Exploding Kitten has already been drawn and defused, so everyone knows it got reinserted somewhere. Player One has been collecting cat cards and still has their Defuse. Player Two wasted their Favor early and is now low on action cards. Player Three is sitting on two Nope cards and not much else.

walkthrough.title: Player One Uses See the Future
walkthrough.text: Smart move from Player One. Before drawing, they play See the Future and peek at the top three cards. The second card is an Exploding Kitten. Player One now plays a Skip card to end their turn without drawing. The danger passes to Player Two. This is exactly how See the Future should be used, right before your draw, so you can react.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: No Protection
walkthrough.text: Player Two has to draw but has no Skip, no Attack, and no See the Future to check what is coming. They draw the first card safely, but remember, Player One saw the Exploding Kitten as the second card. If the deck has not been shuffled, that Kitten is now on top. Player Two draws and pulls the Exploding Kitten. They play their Defuse and survive, reinserting the Kitten secretly into the deck.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: You Must Draw to End Your Turn
walkthrough.text: Some new players forget that drawing is mandatory. You cannot just play action cards and pass without drawing. The only way to avoid drawing is by playing a Skip card or an Attack card. If you have neither, you must draw no matter how scared you are. Plan your hand so you always have an escape option when the deck gets thin.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Nope Problem
walkthrough.text: Player Three has been hoarding Nope cards all game. They have three Nopes but no Skip, no Attack, and no See the Future. Nope cards are great for blocking other players, but they do not help you avoid drawing. Player Three draws a card and it is the Exploding Kitten. They have no Defuse. All those Nope cards are useless because you cannot Nope an Exploding Kitten. Player Three is eliminated.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Hoarding too many Nope cards is a common trap. Nopes are powerful for disrupting opponents, but they do nothing to keep you alive when you draw an Exploding Kitten. You need a mix of defensive cards like Skip and Attack alongside your Nopes. If your hand is all Nopes and cat cards, you are in trouble.

walkthrough.title: The Final Showdown
walkthrough.text: We are down to Player One and Player Two. The deck is tiny now, maybe five or six cards with one Exploding Kitten hiding in there. Every draw is a coin flip. Player One plays See the Future and spots the Kitten on top. They play an Attack card, forcing Player Two to take two turns. Player Two must draw twice with no way to avoid it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Save Attack Cards for the Endgame
walkthrough.text: Wasting Attack cards early when the deck is thick and safe is a rookie mistake. Early in the game, the odds of hitting an Exploding Kitten are tiny, so forcing someone to draw twice is barely a threat. Save your Attacks for the endgame when the deck is thin and every draw could be lethal. That is when Attack cards become devastating.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations and Next Steps
walkthrough.text: You have seen a full practice game of Exploding Kittens! You learned to save your action cards for when the deck gets thin, to always keep a way to avoid drawing, to balance Nopes with actual survival cards, and to time your Attacks for maximum impact. Check out General Tips for more strategy, and use the Appendix as a quick reference for any card you forget. Most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide any question during play. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome to the Table
bullets: Practice game with 3 players, 8-card hands, 2 Exploding Kittens in deck
Follow along to learn timing, hand management, common mistakes

summary.title: Player One's First Turn
bullets: Deck is thick and safe — no reason to play action cards
Draws a card, gets Beard Cat — safe and simple

summary.title: Player Two Gets Greedy
bullets: Plays Favor early to steal a card from Player Three
Gets a mediocre cat card — wasted an action card for low value

summary.title: Lesson: Save Favors for Later
bullets: Favor gives opponent the choice — they dump their worst card
Late-game Favors are stronger: smaller hands, higher-value targets
Save action cards for when they have maximum impact

summary.title: Player Three Draws Safely
bullets: Thick deck = low danger, just draw and save your cards
Early game is about conservation, not spending

summary.title: The Deck Gets Thin

Deck is small — every draw is risky now
Player 2 is low on action cards from early spending

summary.title: Player One Uses See the Future
bullets: Peeks at top 3 → spots Exploding Kitten as card #2
Plays Skip to avoid drawing → danger passes to Player 2
See the Future + Skip/Attack = powerful combo

summary.title: Player Two's Mistake: No Protection
bullets: No Skip, no Attack, no See the Future in hand
Draws the Exploding Kitten, must use Defuse to survive
Burned their only Defuse because they had no escape options

summary.title: Lesson: You Must Draw to End Your Turn
bullets: Drawing is mandatory — only Skip or Attack avoids it
Can't just play cards and pass without drawing
Always keep at least one escape card in hand

summary.title: Player Three's Nope Problem
bullets: 3 Nope cards but no Skip, no Attack, no Defuse
Draws Exploding Kitten — Nopes can't block it → eliminated
Can't Nope an Exploding Kitten or Defuse

summary.title: Lesson: Balance Your Hand
bullets: Nopes disrupt opponents but don't save you from explosions
Need a mix: Nopes + Skip/Attack + Defuse (if possible)
All-Nope hands are a death sentence when the deck thins

summary.title: The Final Showdown
bullets: 2 players, tiny deck, 1 Exploding Kitten lurking
Player 1 uses See the Future → Attack combo to force draws
Every endgame draw is high-stakes

summary.title: Lesson: Save Attack Cards for the Endgame
bullets: Early Attacks = barely a threat (thick deck, low odds)
Late Attacks = devastating (thin deck, high explosion odds)
Same logic applies to all aggressive cards

summary.title: Congratulations and Next Steps
bullets: Save action cards for thin-deck moments
Keep at least one escape card (Skip/Attack) for mandatory draws
Balance Nopes with survival cards
Check General Tips, Appendix, and Q&A for more help

walkthrough.title: Card Conservation
walkthrough.text: The most important skill in Exploding Kittens is knowing when not to play cards. Early in the game, the deck is thick and the odds of drawing an Exploding Kitten are tiny. Every card you play now is a card you will not have later when the deck is thin and deadly. Resist the urge to play action cards just because you can. Draw your card, keep your hand stocked, and wait for the moment when your cards actually matter.

walkthrough.title: Protect Your Defuse
walkthrough.text: Your Defuse card is the single most valuable card in your hand. Never trade it away via Favor if you can avoid it. Never use cat card pairs on someone who might steal it back. If someone plays a Favor on you and you have a Defuse, give them literally anything else. Some players even Nope a Favor card just to protect their Defuse. Losing your Defuse means the next Exploding Kitten you draw is instant elimination.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what other players are doing. If someone just peeked at the deck with See the Future and immediately played a Skip, that means something dangerous is near the top. If a player gets nervous or suddenly starts burning cards, they probably know something you do not. Count the Exploding Kittens that have been drawn and defused. Track who has used their Defuse and who still has protection. Information wins games.

walkthrough.title: Nope Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Nope cards are powerful but they are not free. Every Nope you play is one fewer card in your hand. Use Nopes strategically. Block an Attack card that targets you. Block a Favor aimed at stealing from you. Block someone's Skip when you know the top card is an Exploding Kitten. Do not waste Nopes on moves that do not affect you. Let other players fight each other while you save your resources.

walkthrough.title: Exploding Kitten Placement
walkthrough.text: When you Defuse an Exploding Kitten, where you place it back in the deck is a huge strategic decision. Put it on top to nail the next player immediately. Put it near the top if you want to create short-term chaos. Bury it deep if you want to buy yourself time. If you just used See the Future, you know the top few cards, so you can place the Kitten in the perfect ambush spot. Never reveal where you put it, and try not to give away clues with your body language.

walkthrough.title: The See the Future Combo
walkthrough.text: The strongest play in Exploding Kittens is combining See the Future with another card. Peek at the top three cards. If one is an Exploding Kitten, play a Skip or Attack to avoid drawing it and push it onto the next player. If the top three are all safe, draw confidently and save your other cards. If you do not have a Skip or Attack, play a Shuffle to randomize the danger away. See the Future turns guessing into planning, so use it right before you draw for maximum value.

summary.title: Card Conservation
bullets: Early game: deck is thick, odds of explosion are low
Don't play action cards just because you can
Save everything for the thin-deck danger zone
Drawing and passing is the best early-game strategy

summary.title: Protect Your Defuse
bullets: Defuse = most valuable card in your hand
Never give it away via Favor — sacrifice anything else
Consider Noping a Favor just to protect your Defuse
No Defuse = instant death on next Exploding Kitten draw

summary.title: Read the Table
bullets: See the Future + Skip = danger is near the top
Track who has used their Defuse and who hasn't
Count defused/reinserted Exploding Kittens
Nervous opponents are telegraphing information

summary.title: Nope Card Timing
bullets: Block Attacks/Favors targeting you — high value
Block Skips when top card is an Exploding Kitten — devastating
Don't waste Nopes on moves that don't affect you
Let opponents burn each other's resources

summary.title: Exploding Kitten Placement
bullets: Top = instant trap for next player
Near top = short-term chaos for nearby players
Deep = buy yourself time
Use See the Future info to place the perfect ambush

summary.title: The See the Future Combo
bullets: See the Future → Skip/Attack = dodge and redirect the Kitten
See the Future → safe top 3 = draw confidently, save cards
See the Future → Shuffle = randomize danger away
Always use See the Future right before your draw

walkthrough.title: Counting the Deck
walkthrough.text: Serious players track the deck size throughout the game. Start by counting total cards after setup. Each turn, one card is drawn. When cards are played, they go to the discard pile. If you know there are fifteen cards left and two Exploding Kittens still in the deck, that is a one-in-seven chance of death per draw. When the deck shrinks to six cards with two Kittens, that is one in three. Knowing the odds helps you decide when to burn resources and when to save them.

walkthrough.title: The Defuse Steal
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful plays in the game is stealing a Defuse card from another player. Use a pair of matching cat cards to randomly steal from someone you suspect still has a Defuse. If they have not drawn an Exploding Kitten yet, their Defuse is probably still in their hand. Stealing it gives you a second life while leaving them completely exposed. This move is risky because the steal is random, but when the deck gets thin and the stakes are high, it can win the game.

walkthrough.title: Attack Chain Strategy
walkthrough.text: Attacks are at their deadliest in the endgame, but you can create devastating chains with them. If you Attack someone, they owe two turns. If they Attack the next player, that player now owes four turns. In a thin deck, owing four draws is almost certainly fatal. Save your Attacks for when the deck is small and try to create chains where multiple Attacks stack on a single victim. This is the most aggressive kill strategy in the game.

walkthrough.title: Baiting Nopes
walkthrough.text: If you suspect an opponent has a Nope card and you want to make a critical play, bait their Nope first. Play a low-value card like a Shuffle or a Favor, then wait. If they Nope it, your bait worked and now your important card will resolve. If they let it through, you still get the effect. Advanced players keep one or two throwaway cards in hand specifically for this purpose. The player who controls the Nope economy controls the game.

walkthrough.title: Top-Deck Placement Mindgames
walkthrough.text: When you Defuse an Exploding Kitten, your body language and timing tell a story. If you quickly shove the card somewhere, opponents assume you put it on top. If you take a long time counting cards, they assume you buried it deep. Use this to your advantage. Take a medium amount of time, act like you are thinking hard, and put it wherever serves you best. You can also bluff verbally. Say something like 'I hope you like the top card' even if you buried it near the bottom. Mindgames are half the fun.

walkthrough.title: Triple Cat Card Targeting
walkthrough.text: The triple cat card combo is the most precise tool in the game. You name a specific card and demand it from an opponent. Use it to steal a Defuse from someone you know has one, or to grab a Skip card from someone right before they need it. The key is information. If you saw someone pick up a Defuse from a random steal earlier, you know exactly what to demand. If someone just peeked at the deck and looks relieved, demand their See the Future so they lose their scouting advantage. Triples are rare, so make them count.

summary.title: Counting the Deck
bullets: Track deck size: total cards minus draws minus discards
Calculate odds: Exploding Kittens remaining / cards remaining
Low odds = conserve, high odds = spend resources to survive
Always know how many Exploding Kittens are still in the deck

summary.title: The Defuse Steal
bullets: Cat card pair → random steal from suspected Defuse holder
Double life for you, death sentence for them
Best when target hasn't drawn an Exploding Kitten yet
High risk (random) but game-winning when it hits

summary.title: Attack Chain Strategy
bullets: Attack → 2 draws, Attack the Attack → 4 draws on next player
4 draws in a thin deck is nearly guaranteed death
Save Attacks for endgame when draws are lethal
Most aggressive kill strategy in the game

summary.title: Baiting Nopes
bullets: Play a low-value card first (Shuffle, Favor) to draw out Nopes
If Noped → your important play now resolves freely
If not Noped → you still get the bait card's effect
Controlling the Nope economy = controlling the game

summary.title: Top-Deck Placement Mindgames
bullets: Body language and timing telegraph placement — use it to bluff
Quick insert → opponents assume top; slow insert → assume deep
Verbal bluffs: say 'enjoy the top card' while burying it deep
Mindgames are as important as card play

summary.title: Triple Cat Card Targeting
bullets: Name a specific card + demand from opponent (must give if held)
Use info: steal known Defuses, Skips, or See the Futures
Triples are rare — make them count on high-value targets
Best when you have confirmed info about opponent's hand

entries.definition: The core danger card. If drawn, you must play a Defuse card immediately or you are eliminated. Cannot be Noped. Four in the deck, but only (players minus one) are inserted during setup.

Saves you when you draw an Exploding Kitten. Play it to survive, discard the Defuse, then secretly reinsert the Exploding Kitten anywhere in the draw pile. Six in the deck; each player starts with one, extras are shuffled in.
Ends your turn without drawing. The next player must take two turns in a row. Attacks stack — if the next player is already under Attack, they owe additional turns. Four in the deck.
Ends your turn immediately without drawing a card. If you owe multiple turns from an Attack, Skip only cancels one of them. Four in the deck.
Force any other player to give you one card from their hand. The targeted player chooses which card to give. Four in the deck.
Randomize the entire draw pile. Useful after someone reinserts an Exploding Kitten or when See the Future reveals danger on top. Four in the deck.
Privately peek at the top three cards of the draw pile without changing their order. Best used immediately before your draw so you can react with Skip, Attack, or Shuffle. Five in the deck.

Cancel any action card played by any player. Can be played at any time, even on other players' turns. Cannot cancel Exploding Kittens or Defuse cards. Nopes can chain — odd number cancels, even number resolves. Five in the deck.

Five types of cat cards (Taco Cat, Hairy Potato Cat, Beard Cat, Cattermelon, Rainbow Ralphing Cat) with four copies each, totaling twenty cards. No individual power. Used in matching pairs or triples for steal combos.
Play two matching cat cards to steal one random card from any opponent. The opponent fans their hand face down and you pick blindly. Works with any two cards of the same type.
Play three matching cat cards to name a specific card and demand it from any opponent. If they have it, they must hand it over. If not, you get nothing. The most precise stealing tool in the game.
Taking the top card from the draw pile. Mandatory at the end of every turn unless avoided by Skip or Attack. Drawing an Exploding Kitten triggers the Defuse-or-die sequence.
When you draw an Exploding Kitten without a Defuse, you are out of the game. Discard all your cards and sit out. The game continues until only one player remains.

When multiple Nope cards are played in sequence on the same action. An odd total number of Nopes cancels the original action. An even total number means the action resolves normally. Any player can add to the chain.
When an Attack is played on a player who already owes extra turns from a previous Attack, the turn count increases. The targeted player owes the remaining turns plus two more from the new Attack.

After Defusing an Exploding Kitten, you secretly place it back anywhere in the draw pile. Other players cannot see where you put it. This is a major strategic decision — top placement targets the next player, deep placement buys time.
There is no hand limit in Exploding Kittens. You can hold as many cards as you want. This encourages conservation — saving cards for critical moments rather than spending them early.

Each turn has two phases: an optional action phase where you may play any number of cards, followed by a mandatory draw phase. The draw phase ends your turn. Skip and Attack cards allow you to bypass the draw.
The win condition of Exploding Kittens. There is no scoring and no points. The game continues until all players except one have been eliminated by Exploding Kittens. The sole survivor wins.
